question: Appendix
answer:
UCSB Field Experience Template for Paper 2: Discovering Love at UCSB
Directions: Use this template to guide your UCSB field observations investigating how love (broadly defined: romantic love, friendship, community care, mentorship, devotion) manifests in our campus community. 
Fill in as many details as possible. Please handwrite your notes as this will make you a more active observer and involved participant.
You will download/make a copy and also submit this completed template with your Essay 2 draft.

Name: Esteban Wu
Location: UCSB campus (dining commons, Lagoon, walkway near offices, lecture hall)
Date/Time:  13 May(11am-4pm)
Duration: 2+ hours

 
Quick Observation Log
Use this template to capture 4-5 moments. Each moment will become one Telar story.
 
Moment 1(Dining Commons)
📸 Photo taken?  Yes
 Time: 1:00 PM
🟦 OBSERVE - What happened? (Just describe - like a camera)
Two friends sat across from each other at a window table in a UCSB Carillo dining commons, sharing one phone to watch a TV show. Both leaned slightly forward toward the small screen and did not speak the whole time I watched.

🟩 INTERPRET - What type of love is this?
Friendship 
Why does it matter?
It shows love does not always need conversation; shared attention and physical closeness can be enough.
 
🟨 CONNECT - Which Unit 2 reading relates?
Article: “The Brain on Love” by Diane Ackerman
Concept/idea: Love as a neurological/emotional experience
How it connects: Ackerman argues that physical closeness with a loved one lowers stress and creates a sense of safety. These two friends sharing one screen in silence shows how shared focus and quiet closeness produce the same calm bond, even without words.
🟥 VISUAL DETAILS - What in the photo shows this?
Detail 1: Both friends are leaning toward the same small phone screen
Detail 2: Complete silence between them despite the busy dining hall around
 
Moment 2(Lagoon Photo Shoot)
📸 Photo taken?  Yes
 Time: 3:30 PM
🟦 OBSERVE:
At the UCSB Lagoon, one student crouched on a path with a professional camera, taking photos of his friend, who posed near the water. They worked together carefully, with the photographer giving small directions.
🟩 INTERPRET:
Type: Friendship/Passion.
Why it matters: Helping a friend with something that matters to them, like getting good photos, is a small, generous act that builds friendship over time.
🟨 CONNECT:
Article: “The Secret to Love is Just Kindness” by Emily Esfahani Smith 
Concept: Love as everyday acts of kindness 
Connection: Smith argues that strong relationships are built through small acts of kindness and “turning toward” a friend’s needs or interests. This scene shows the same idea in friendship; the photographer turns toward his friend by giving his time, attention, and effort to capture meaningful photos. Helping a friend with something they care about is exactly the kind of everyday kindness Smith describes 
🟥 VISUAL DETAILS:
Detail 1: One friend crouched low with a professional camera, focused on the shot 
Detail 2: The other friend is posing in a carefully way.
 
Moment 3 (Walkway Conversation)
📸 Photo taken?  Yes
 Time: 2:00 PM
🟦 OBSERVE:
On a covered walkway near campus offices, a student with a backpack stood facing an older woman who looked like a mentor or faculty member. They talked and smiled, fully focused on each other. The student leaned slightly forward, listening.
🟩 INTERPRET:
Type: Mentorship  
Why: Love and care on campus also come from mentor relationships: faculty, advisors, or older friends. Not only from peers or romantic partners.
🟨 CONNECT:
Article: “To Fall in Love With Anyone, Do This” by Mandy Len Catron 
 Concept: Love created through vulnerability and deep conversation 
 Connection: Catron argues that closeness grows when two people give real time to listen. This mentor moment shows the same idea. Taking time to talk openly with someone older builds emotional connection and trust outside romantic love.
🟥 VISUAL DETAILS:
1.	The student is leaning slightly toward the mentor while listening  
2.	Both fully facing each other with no phones or distractions
 
Moment 4(Lecture Hall)
📸 Photo taken? 
 Time: 10:00 AM
🟦 OBSERVE:
Inside a UCSB lecture hall, dozens of students sat quietly facing the same direction, sharing attention on the same lecture. The room was calm and focused, with students seated close together.
🟩 INTERPRET:
Type: Community care 
 Why: Quietly sharing a learning space and respecting each other’s focus is a small but real form of everyday kindness in a campus community.
🟨 CONNECT:
Article: “The Secret to Love is Just Kindness” by Emily Esfahani Smith
Concept: Love as everyday acts of kindness on a community scale 
 Connection: Smith argues that kindness is the foundation of strong relationships and grows through small, repeated acts. A lecture hall shows this on a community level, dozens of students share space, stay quiet, and respect each other’s focus. These almost invisible acts of consideration are exactly the kind of everyday kindness Smith says holds people together over time.

🟥 VISUAL DETAILS:
1.	All students are facing the same direction, focused on the same speaker
2.	Students are seated close to each other without tension, sharing the same space comfortably
 
Brief Reflection (200-300 words)
This field experience taught me that love at UCSB exists in many small, everyday forms that I usually pass by without noticing. Before this assignment, I mostly thought of love as romantic. After spending over an hour observing on campus, I realized that love also shows up as friendship, mentorship, shared learning, and quiet companionship.
The four moments I observed connect to three different Unit 2 readings in clear ways. The two friends sharing a phone in the dining commons reminded me of Diane Ackerman’s argument that love is a neurological and emotional experience, not only an idea. Their silence and shared focus showed how physical closeness creates comfort. The lagoon photo shoot and the lecture hall are both connected to Emily Esfahani Smith’s idea that love is built through small, everyday acts of kindness. Helping a friend get good photos and quietly respecting a shared learning space are both examples of “turning toward” others through small, generous behavior. The conversation between a student and her mentor is connected to Mandy Len Catron’s idea that love grows through deep conversation and giving time to listen.
For my Telar exhibition, I will include the dining commons photo and the walkway conversation photo because they capture two very different sides of love at UCSB. One quiet and emotional conversation between friends, the other built through a deep conversation with a mentor. I want viewers to see that love at our campus is not only one thing. It exists in shared silence, in small acts of kindness, in mentorship, and in the simple act of choosing to share space and time with other people.

Certification
☐ I spent 1+ hour observing
 ☐ I took 4-5 photos
 ☐ These are my own observations
 ☐ No AI was used or if it was used to revise for any grammar, word choice, or sentence structures, it is acknowledged ethically with an accompanying reflection and by completing our Using AI as a Tutor Guide
